1: Yesh ra'ah asher ra'iti tahat hashemesh ve-rabbah hi al-ha'adam — 'There is an evil I have observed under the sun, and it is widespread among human beings.' The Teacher introduces a new case study with his characteristic empirical formula (ra'iti, I have seen). Ve-rabbah hi al-ha'adam — 'it is widespread/heavy upon people' — the adjective rabbah (H7231, great/widespread/heavy) describes both the frequency of the evil and its weight on those who experience it. The evil of Ecclesiastes 6 is more refined than oppression (Ecclesiastes 5): it is the situation of having everything materially and being unable to enjoy any of it.
2: Ish asher yiten-lo ha'Elohim osher u-nekhasim ve-khavod — 'Here is a man to whom God gives wealth, riches, and honor.' The triple gift (wealth, riches, honor) is the complete package of material and social blessing. Ve-einennu machsor le-nafsho mi-kol asher yit'aveh — 'so that nothing he desires is lacking.' The word nefesh (H5315) here means appetite/desire — what the soul craves. Ve-lo-yashlitennu ha'Elohim le'ekhol mimmennu — 'yet God does not give him the capacity to enjoy any of it.' The verb shalat (dominion/control) appeared at 5:19 for the one God enables; its negation here is the condition of the man who has everything but the ability to experience it. Ki ish nokri yokhlennu — 'and a stranger devours it all' — the total loss of inheritance, as in Ecclesiastes 2:18-19 but now the stranger may arrive even before death.
3: Im-yolid ish meah ve-shanim rabbat yich'yeh — 'A man may father a hundred children and live a long life.' The extremes of apparent blessing: one hundred children (far beyond the patriarchal ideal) and many years. Ve-nefesh lo-tisbah min-hatovah — 'yet if he cannot fill himself with good things' — sava (to be satisfied/full) with the good: the insatiability from Ecclesiastes 1:7-8 reappears. Ve-gam-keverah lo-hayta lo — 'and in the end receives no burial' — burial denial was in the ANE the ultimate dishonor; it meant no memorial, no rites, no continuing presence in the community's memory. Even with one hundred children and long life, without enjoyment and without proper burial, the stillborn (nefesh) fares better.
4: Ki be-hevel ba u-ve-choshekh yelekh — 'For the stillborn comes in vapor and departs in darkness.' Be-hevel ba — 'comes in hevel' — the stillborn's brief, breath-like existence is itself an instance of hevel. U-ve-choshekh yelekh — 'and goes in darkness' — the stillborn never sees light (Ecclesiastes 6:5). U-ve-choshekh shemo yekhuseh — 'and its name is buried in darkness' — shemo yekhuseh (its name is covered/buried) echoes the memorial concern of Ecclesiastes 1:11: no zikaron, no name, no memory. The stillborn's condition is the Teacher's standard for ultimate deprivation — and even this is better than the man with one hundred children who cannot enjoy them.
5: Gam-shemesh lo-ra'ah ve-lo yada — 'It has never seen the sun or known anything.' The stillborn's deprivations accumulate: no light, no knowledge, no experience. Nachath la-zeh min-zeh — 'yet it finds more rest than the other.' The word nachath (H5183, rest/contentment) appeared at 4:6 for the one hand of quietness that is better than two hands of toil. The stillborn's total deprivation paradoxically constitutes rest — it has escaped the impossible burden of having everything without the capacity to enjoy it.
6: Ve-ilu chayah alef shanim pa'amayim ve-tovah lo ra'ah — 'Even if that man were to live two thousand years and yet find nothing good.' The two-thousand-year hypothetical extends the failure to its maximum possible term: not one hundred children and many years, but two thousand years — and still no enjoyment. Halo el-maqom echad haKol holekh — 'does not everyone go to the same place in the end?' The rhetorical question returns to the maqom echad (one place) of Ecclesiastes 3:20 — death as the universal destination that erases the distinction between the advantaged and the disadvantaged. Even two thousand years without enjoyment converge on the same terminus as a brief life with it.
7: Kol-amal ha'adam le-fihu ve-gam ha-nefesh lo timale — 'All of a person's labor goes toward filling the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied.' Le-fihu (for his mouth) — the mouth as the primary organ of consumption; all labor is directed toward the basic act of eating. Ha-nefesh lo timale — 'the appetite is never satisfied' — nefesh here in its appetite sense (as in Ecclesiastes 6:2). The same sava/fullness that the sea never reaches (Ecclesiastes 1:7) and the eye never achieves (Ecclesiastes 1:8) now describes the appetite's fundamental insatiability. The structure is circular: labor feeds the mouth, the mouth drives more labor, the appetite is never filled.
8: Ki mah-yitron le-chakham min-ha-kesil — 'What real advantage does the wise person have over the fool?' Yitron recurs in its comparative form: if the appetite of both wise and fool is equally insatiable, and if both die the same death, what does wisdom's advantage amount to? Mah-le'ani yode'a lahalekh neged ha-chayyim — 'and what does the poor person gain from knowing how to navigate life?' The phrase lahalekh neged ha-chayyim (to walk before/against the living, or to navigate life) is a practical wisdom phrase: even the practical skill of survival in society produces no lasting advantage for the poor person. The question undercuts any easy equation of wisdom with success.
9: Tov mar'eh einayim mi-halokh-nefesh — 'Better what the eyes can actually see than the restless wandering of desire.' Mar'eh einayim (what the eyes see) — the immediate, present experience. Halokh nefesh (wandering of the soul/desire) — the restless movement of appetite toward what is not possessed. The tov min comparison counsels present enjoyment over restless desire: what is actually before one is better than what desire drives one toward. Gam-zeh hevel u-re'ut ruach — the full verdict formula. Even this observation — and the practical wisdom it contains — falls under the hevel category within the under-the-sun frame.
10: Mah-shehayah kebar niqra shemo — 'Whatever exists has already been named.' The naming of what exists picks up Genesis 2:19-20 (Adam naming the animals) — to be named is to be placed within the created order. Ve-noda asher-hu adam — 'it is known what a human being is' — the essence of the human (adam) is already established; there is no novelty in the human condition. Ve-lo yuchal ladin im sheteqef mimmennu — 'and no one can contend with one far more powerful.' The word teqef (H8624, more powerful/stronger) likely refers to God — the human cannot dispute with the One who has established the order. This is the epistemic limit of 'under the sun' existence: the order is given, not negotiable.
11: Ki yesh-devarim harbeh marbim havel — 'The more words there are, the more futility.' Marbim havel (multiplying hevel) — the paradox: the more one speaks to explain existence, the more futility is generated. The Teacher has been speaking at length about futility; even his own commentary participates in the multiplication of hevel-words. Mah-yoter le'adam — 'what benefit is there for a person?' The question leaves the reader precisely where the Teacher intends: without a satisfying answer within the 'under the sun' frame.
12: Ki mi yode'a mah-tov le'adam ba-chayyim — 'For who knows what is truly good for a person in life?' The mi yode'a ('who knows?') formula marks the epistemological limit of this section. Mispar yemei-chayyei havlecha — 'during all those fleeting days' — literally 'the number of the days of his vapor-life.' The genitival phrase chayyei havlecha (the life of your vapor/futility) is striking: life itself is characterized as hevel — not the individual moments but the whole of existence under the sun. Ve-ashem yagid le-adam mah-yihyeh acharav tahat hashemesh — 'and who can tell anyone what will happen under the sun after they are gone?' The double limitation: no one knows what is good now; no one knows what will happen after. The epistemological frame of 'under the sun' wisdom is completely closed from both ends.
